On-demand; iterative (re-dispatch to break a still-hard sub-lemma further, down to sentence level).

10	    one go — a high-`effort_local` node, or one the progress-critic flags as a
11	    repeated blocker. I am most valuable on a **frontier** node (deps already
12	    done): it is the live bottleneck, and decomposing it exposes small ready
13	    pieces next iter.
14	  - Name the target label in `## Target`, and say **how aggressively to break**
15	    in `## Granularity`: "one level" (the proof's main steps) by default, or
16	    "fine — one mathematical claim per lemma" when a prior, coarser break still
17	    left a hard piece. Decomposition is ITERATIVE and has no floor: if a break
18	    doesn't drop the difficulty enough, re-dispatch me on the still-hard
19	    sub-lemma asking me to break it further (sentence-by-sentence, like the
20	    fine-grained prover). Do not pre-empt this — just break, see if it helped,
21	    break more if not.
22	  - Give me the proof sketch / reference structure to cut along in
23	    `## Proof structure` so I split at real mathematical seams, not arbitrary
24	    points.
25	  - Do NOT dispatch me at an already-done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) — there
26	    is nothing to break. Do NOT dispatch me at an ∞-effort node (no informal
27	    proof to decompose) — send a dag-walker / blueprint-writer to write the

32	
33	# Effort Breaker
34	
35	You take **one high-effort blueprint declaration** and split it into a `\uses{}`-linked chain of **smaller, easier sub-lemmas**, so the prover formalizes a sequence of small goals instead of one large one. The target's proof becomes "by `L1`, `L2`, …, `L_n`", each `L_i` is a new block with its own statement, informal proof, and `\uses{}`, and each `L_i` is strictly easier than the original.
36	
37	You decompose DOWN (one node → many smaller ones). This is the complement of the dag-walker (which fills the cone UP). You do not invent new mathematics — you re-express the target's existing proof as a chain of named intermediate results.
38	
39	## Directive Format
40	
41	```markdown
42	# Effort Breaker Directive
43	
44	## Slug
45	<slug>
46	
47	## Target
48	<the blueprint label to break, e.g. thm:flattening_stratification_exists>
49	
50	## Granularity
51	<"one level" (the proof's main steps) | "fine — one mathematical claim per
52	 lemma" (when a coarser break already happened and a piece is still hard)>
53	
54	## Proof structure
55	<the seams to cut along: the steps of the target's informal proof, or the
56	 structure of the source proof. The dispatcher gives you this so you split at
57	 real mathematical boundaries.>
58	
59	## Strategy context
60	<the slice of STRATEGY.md that matters — what this result supports. You do NOT
61	 read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
62	
63	## References
64	- <path/to/reference.md>: <which proof / steps live here>
65	```
66	
67	## How you break
68	
69	1. Read the target block on disk and inspect it: `archon dag-query node --node <target> --json` (effort, deps, used-by). `archon` is on PATH; `--json` is parseable (banner on stderr).
70	2. Read the target's informal proof (and the source proof it cites, per the directive's references). Identify the **steps** — each self-contained claim the proof establishes on the way to the conclusion.
71	3. For each step, author a new sub-lemma block in the appropriate chapter:
72	   - a `\label{lem:<descriptive>}` and a `\lean{...}` hint (the Lean name the prover will use; name it by convention if the directive doesn't give one, and note it for the plan agent),
73	   - the **statement** of that step in the project's notation,
74	   - an informal `\begin{proof}` for the step with `\uses{...}` naming its own dependencies (existing blocks, Mathlib anchors, or earlier sub-lemmas in this chain),
75	   - citation discipline (below) when the step derives from a source.
76	4. **Rewrite the target's proof** to invoke the chain: a short proof body that combines `L1 … L_n`, with `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}` (plus any direct deps that remain). The target keeps its statement and `\lean{}` unchanged.
77	5. Each `L_i` must be **strictly smaller** than the target (smaller proof, fewer moving parts). If a step is still large, either break it into sub-steps now (at fine granularity) or flag it in the report so the dispatcher can re-break it next round.
78	
79	Granularity is the directive's call. "One level" = the proof's main steps. "Fine" = one mathematical assertion per lemma — the limit is the same as the fine-grained prover: a lemma so small its proof is a single move. There is no floor; if a break didn't help, the next dispatch breaks further.
80	
81	## Markers and citation (same rules as every blueprint agent)
82	
83	- **Never add `\leanok`** — it is the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase's job.
84	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor** — when a step is a result Mathlib already provides, write it as an anchor (`\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}`, `\mathlibok`, `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}`, no proof) rather than re-proving it. Mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote — a wrong anchor is worse than leaving the step to prove.
85	- **Citation discipline (hard rule):** every block derived from a source needs `% SOURCE:` (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a verbatim original-language `% SOURCE QUOTE:` copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` first line. Never write these from memory; if you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait, read the new file, then write the cited block. Never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
86	
87	## Rules
88	
89	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). No `.lean`, no `content.tex`, no `macros/`. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
90	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces).
91	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no project-history narrative.
92	- **Preserve the target's statement and `\lean{}`.** You re-express its *proof* as a chain; you do not weaken or restate the theorem. (If the target's statement looks wrong, do NOT silently fix it — flag it under "Notes" as a strategy item.)
93	- **Conserve the mathematics.** The chain must actually prove the target — every gap the original proof crossed is covered by some `L_i`. Don't drop a hard step by relabelling it; a step you cannot decompose or prove is reported, not hidden.
94	- **Verify with the graph.** After breaking, run `archon dag-query node --node <target>` and `archon dag-query ancestors --node <target>`: the target's effort should drop, the new `L_i` should appear, and there must be no broken `\uses{}`. Fix and re-check.
95	
96	## Workflow
97	
98	1. Read the directive and the target block on disk.
99	2. Read the references the directive names; identify the proof's steps.
100	3. Author the sub-lemma chain (statement + proof + `\uses` for each), bottom-up so later lemmas can `\uses{}` earlier ones.
101	4. Rewrite the target's proof to invoke the chain.
102	5. For any step needing a source you lack, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
103	6. Re-query the graph; confirm the target's effort dropped and no `\uses{}` broke.
104	7. Write your report.
105	
106	## Logging
107	
108	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/effort-breaker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).
109	
110	```markdown
111	# Effort Breaker Report
112	
113	## Slug
114	<slug>
115	
116	## Target
117	<label>
118	
119	## Status
120	<COMPLETE — target re-expressed as a chain | PARTIAL — a step could not be broken/proved (listed below)>
121	
122	## Effort before → after
123	- target `effort_local`: <before> → <after>
124	- sub-lemmas added: <count>
125	
126	## Chain added (target ← L_n ← … ← L1)
127	- `\label{lem:L1}` `\lean{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` — <statement, one line> (effort ≈ <small>)
128	- `\label{lem:L2}` … — <statement> (\uses{lem:L1})
129	- ...
130	- Target `<label>` proof rewritten: `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}`.
131	
132	## Still hard (re-break candidates)
133	- `lem:Lk` — still large; re-dispatch the breaker at finer granularity, OR <why it's irreducible>.
134	
135	## Could not decompose (strategy items)
136	- <step> — <why: genuinely atomic / novel / statement looks wrong>.
137	
138	## References consulted
139	- `references/<file>.md` — <verbatim content taken>.
140	
141	## Notes for dispatcher
142	- `\lean{}` names I assigned by convention (confirm/scaffold): <list>.
143	- needed macros / cross-chapter concerns.
144	```
145	
146	## Return Value
147	
148	Your final assistant message: one line — `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL — <one-sentence outcome>` — plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

981	\begin{lemma}\leanok
982	[Cramer-inverse cocycle on the triple overlap]
983	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}
984	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle_matrix}
985	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
986	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
987	    lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_cocycle}
988	  Over the common triple-overlap ring (the localisation carrying the three charts'
989	  data to \(V_{IJK}\)) the Cramer inverses of the localised minors satisfy the
990	  multiplicative cocycle identity
991	  \[
992	    (X^J_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}.
993	  \]
994	  This is the pure matrix-algebra core of (C2), independent of any sheaf data.
995	\end{lemma}
996	\begin{proof}
997	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
998	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
999	    lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_cocycle}
1000	  Write all matrices over the common triple-overlap ring. The image matrix of the
1001	  transition \(\theta_{I,J}\) is \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\), and its \(K\)-minor is
1002	  computed by restricting to the \(K\)-indexed columns; since taking a column
1003	  submatrix commutes with left multiplication (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}),
1004	  \[
1005	    X^J_K \;=\; \bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr)_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_K .
1006	  \]
1007	  Both \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\) have unit determinant
1008	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so this product of invertibles inverts
1009	  by \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\):
1010	  \[
1011	    (X^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J).
1012	  \]
1013	  Multiplying on the right by \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) and cancelling \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1\)
1014	  via the two-sided Cramer identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}, the
1015	  cancellation packaged in \cref{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}) gives
1016	  \((X^J_K)^{-1} (X^I_J)^{-1} = (X^I_K)^{-1}\). The agreement of the three image
1017	  matrices over the triple overlap that licenses these manipulations is the content of
1018	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}, the matrix form of the scheme-level cocycle
1019	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}.
1020	\end{proof}

1022	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1023	[Scalar endomorphism is natural under pullback]
1024	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}
1025	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_pullback}
1026	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1027	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(a \in \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) a
1028	  global function, with comorphism on global sections
1029	  \(p^{\sharp} : \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\).
1030	  Write \(q : p^{*}\mathbf{1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathbf{1}\) for the unit-pullback comparison
1031	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index). Then the pullback of the scalar endomorphism
1032	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\) is conjugate, through \(q\), to the scalar endomorphism of the
1033	  base-changed function:
1034	  \[
1035	    p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\bigr)
1036	      \;=\; q^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(p^{\sharp} a\bigr) \,\circ\, q
1037	  \]
1038	  as endomorphisms of \(p^{*}\mathbf{1}\); equivalently
1039	  \(q \circ p^{*}(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(p^{\sharp} a) \circ q\). This is the
1040	  single-entry naturality atom underlying the matrix-level statement
1041	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}.
1042	\end{lemma}
1043	\begin{proof}
1044	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1045	  The scalar endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is multiplication
1046	  by the global section \(a\) on the unit module \(\mathbf 1 = \mathcal{O}_S\). Pulling back
1047	  along \(p\) sends multiplication by \(a\) to multiplication by its image, and the comorphism
1048	  \(p^{\sharp}\) is precisely the action of pullback on global functions, so
1049	  \(p^{*}(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a))\) is multiplication by \(p^{\sharp}a\) on \(p^{*}\mathbf 1\). The
1050	  comparison \(q\) is an isomorphism of unit modules intertwining this multiplication on
1051	  \(p^{*}\mathbf 1\) with multiplication by \(p^{\sharp}a\) on \(\mathbf 1\); conjugating back
1052	  along \(q\) therefore yields the claimed identity. The verification is a single computation on
1053	  the unit module, where multiplication by a function commutes with the natural comparison
1054	  \(q\).
1055	\end{proof}

1057	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1058	[Matrix endomorphism is natural under pullback]
1059	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}
1060	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_pullback}
1061	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1062	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
1063	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), and
1064	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\). Let
1065	  \(p^{\sharp}M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T))\) be the entrywise
1066	  image of \(M\) under the comorphism \(p^{\sharp}\), and let
1067	  \(Q : p^{*}(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) be the free-pullback
1068	  comparison \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at the rank-\(d\) index set. Then the
1069	  pullback of the matrix endomorphism \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is conjugate, through \(Q\), to
1070	  the matrix endomorphism of the base-changed matrix:
1071	  \[
1072	    p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\bigr)
1073	      \;=\; Q^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(p^{\sharp}M) \,\circ\, Q .
1074	  \]
1075	\end{lemma}
1076	\begin{proof}
1077	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1078	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
1079	  By \cref{def:gr_matrixEnd}, \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is the biproduct-assembled endomorphism
1080	  of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} = \bigoplus_{k} \mathbf 1\) whose \((k,\ell)\)-component is
1081	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{k,\ell})\). The pullback functor \(p^{*}\) is a left adjoint, hence
1082	  additive and biproduct-preserving; through the coproduct comparison underlying the
1083	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} it carries the biproduct presentation
1084	  of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) to that of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) and the assembled morphism to the
1085	  morphism reassembled from the pulled-back components, the reassociation of the iterated
1086	  pullbacks being \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. On each single \((k,\ell)\)-component the
1087	  pulled-back scalar endomorphism is, by \cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}, the
1088	  comparison-conjugate of \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(p^{\sharp}M_{k,\ell}) =
1089	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}((p^{\sharp}M)_{k,\ell})\). Reassembling over the biproduct, the conjugating
1090	  one-element comparisons combine into the single free comparison \(Q\), giving
1091	  \(p^{*}(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)) = Q^{-1}\circ\mathrm{matrixEnd}(p^{\sharp}M)\circ Q\).
1092	\end{proof}

284	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — triple-overlap rings and the cocycle
285	
286	The cocycle condition `θ_{I,K} = θ_{I,J} ∘ θ_{J,K}` cannot be stated as a naive
287	composition of the landed `transitionMap`s: the codomain of `transitionMap d r J K`
288	(`R^J[1/P^J_K]`) differs from the domain of `transitionMap d r I J` (`R^J[1/P^J_I]`).
289	The identity therefore lives over the *triple-overlap* rings obtained by inverting BOTH
290	relevant minors in each chart ring:
291	`S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)]`, `S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]`, `S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)]`.
292	We build the localised transition maps `Θ_{I,J} : S_J →+* S_I`, `Θ_{J,K} : S_K →+* S_J`,
293	`Θ_{I,K} : S_K →+* S_I` over these rings and verify the cocycle.
294	
295	Blueprint reference: `lem:gr_cocycle` (`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_GrassmannianCells.tex`). -/
296	
297	/-- A ring homomorphism carries the nonsingular (Cramer) inverse to the nonsingular
298	inverse, provided the determinant is a unit. Project-local helper: Mathlib has no direct
299	`map`-`nonsingular inverse` compatibility lemma. -/
300	private lemma map_nonsing_inv {n : ℕ} {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [CommRing S] (f : R →+* S)
301	    (A : Matrix (Fin n) (Fin n) R) (h : IsUnit A.det) :
302	    (A.map f)⁻¹ = A⁻¹.map f := by
303	  have hmul : (A.map f) * (A⁻¹.map f) = 1 := by
304	    rw [← Matrix.map_mul, Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv A h, Matrix.map_one f (map_zero f) (map_one f)]
305	  exact Matrix.inv_eq_right_inv hmul
306	
307	/-- Inclusion of the away-localisation at `x` into the away-localisation at `x * y`
308	(inverting the extra factor `y`). Project-local: the structure map of the triple overlap
309	relative to a double-localisation. -/
310	noncomputable def awayInclLeft {R : Type*} [CommRing R] (x y : R) :
311	    Localization.Away x →+* Localization.Away (x * y) :=
312	  IsLocalization.Away.lift (S := Localization.Away x) x
313	    (g := algebraMap R (Localization.Away (x * y)))
314	    (by
315	      have h : IsUnit (algebraMap R (Localization.Away (x * y)) (x * y)) :=
316	        IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit _
317	      rw [map_mul] at h
318	      exact isUnit_of_mul_isUnit_left h)
319	
320	/-- Inclusion of the away-localisation at `y` into the away-localisation at `x * y`
321	(inverting the extra factor `x`). Project-local. -/
322	noncomputable def awayInclRight {R : Type*} [CommRing R] (x y : R) :
323	    Localization.Away y →+* Localization.Away (x * y) :=
324	  IsLocalization.Away.lift (S := Localization.Away y) y
325	    (g := algebraMap R (Localization.Away (x * y)))
326	    (by
327	      have h : IsUnit (algebraMap R (Localization.Away (x * y)) (x * y)) :=
328	        IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit _
329	      rw [map_mul] at h
330	      exact isUnit_of_mul_isUnit_right h)

333	Project-local. -/
334	lemma awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap {R : Type*} [CommRing R] (x y : R) :
335	    (awayInclLeft x y).comp (algebraMap R (Localization.Away x)) =
336	      algebraMap R (Localization.Away (x * y)) := by
337	  rw [awayInclLeft]; exact IsLocalization.Away.lift_comp x _
338	
339	/-- `awayInclRight` is the canonical map over `R`: it intertwines the two structure maps.
340	Project-local. -/
341	lemma awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap {R : Type*} [CommRing R] (x y : R) :
342	    (awayInclRight x y).comp (algebraMap R (Localization.Away y)) =
343	      algebraMap R (Localization.Away (x * y)) := by
344	  rw [awayInclRight]; exact IsLocalization.Away.lift_comp y _

346	/-- The image of a general minor determinant `P^B_C` under the pre-localisation hom
347	`θ̃_{A,B}` is the determinant of the `C`-minor of the image matrix `M = (X^A_B)⁻¹ X^A`
348	(generalises the `C = A` computation underlying `lem:gr_transition_pre_unit`). Project-local. -/
349	theorem transitionPreMap_minorDet (d r : ℕ) (I J K : Finset (Fin r)) (hI : I.card = d)
350	    (hJ : J.card = d) (hK : K.card = d) :
351	    transitionPreMap d r I J hI hJ (minorDet d r J K hJ hK)
352	      = ((imageMatrix d r I J hI hJ).submatrix id
353	          (fun j : Fin d => (K.orderIsoOfFin hK j : Fin r))).det := by
354	  have e1 : transitionPreMap d r I J hI hJ (minorDet d r J K hJ hK)
355	      = (((universalMatrix d r J hJ).submatrix id
356	          (fun j : Fin d => (K.orderIsoOfFin hK j : Fin r))).map
357	            (transitionPreMap d r I J hI hJ)).det :=
358	    RingHom.map_det (transitionPreMap d r I J hI hJ).toRingHom _
359	  rw [e1, ← Matrix.submatrix_map, universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap]

361	/-- The "cross" minor `P^B_C` is sent by `θ̃_{A,B}`, then pushed into a double localisation
362	`D` (in which `P^A_C` is a unit), to a unit. Concretely
363	`θ̃_{A,B}(P^B_C) = det((X^A_B)⁻¹ X^A_C) = det((X^A_B)⁻¹) · P^A_C`, a product of two units
364	once `P^A_C` is inverted. This is the cross-factor input to each triple-overlap transition
365	lift. Project-local. -/
366	private lemma isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross
367	    (d r : ℕ) (A B C : Finset (Fin r)) (hA : A.card = d) (hB : B.card = d) (hC : C.card = d)
368	    {D : Type*} [CommRing D] [Algebra (MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ A}) ℤ) D]
369	    (incl : Localization.Away (minorDet d r A B hA hB) →+* D)
370	    (hincl : incl.comp (algebraMap (MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ A}) ℤ)
371	        (Localization.Away (minorDet d r A B hA hB)))
372	        = algebraMap (MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ A}) ℤ) D)
373	    (hunit : IsUnit (algebraMap (MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ A}) ℤ) D
374	        (minorDet d r A C hA hC))) :
375	    IsUnit (incl (transitionPreMap d r A B hA hB (minorDet d r B C hB hC))) := by
376	  have hsub : (imageMatrix d r A B hA hB).submatrix id
377	        (fun j : Fin d => (C.orderIsoOfFin hC j : Fin r))
378	      = universalMinorInv d r A B hA hB *
379	        (((universalMatrix d r A hA).map (algebraMap _ _)).submatrix id
380	          (fun j : Fin d => (C.orderIsoOfFin hC j : Fin r))) := mul_submatrix_col _ _ _
381	  rw [transitionPreMap_minorDet, hsub, Matrix.det_mul, map_mul]
382	  refine IsUnit.mul ?_ ?_
383	  · refine IsUnit.map incl ?_
384	    refine IsUnit.of_mul_eq_one (universalMinor d r A B hA hB).det ?_
385	    rw [← Matrix.det_mul, (universalMinorInv_mul_cancel d r A B hA hB).1, Matrix.det_one]
386	  · have hdet : (((universalMatrix d r A hA).map
387	            (algebraMap _ (Localization.Away (minorDet d r A B hA hB)))).submatrix id
388	            (fun j : Fin d => (C.orderIsoOfFin hC j : Fin r))).det
389	          = algebraMap _ (Localization.Away (minorDet d r A B hA hB)) (minorDet d r A C hA hC) := by
390	      rw [Matrix.submatrix_map]
391	      exact (RingHom.map_det _ _).symm
392	    rw [hdet]
393	    have hcomp : incl (algebraMap (MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ A}) ℤ)
394	        (Localization.Away (minorDet d r A B hA hB)) (minorDet d r A C hA hC))
395	        = algebraMap (MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ A}) ℤ) D (minorDet d r A C hA hC) :=
396	      RingHom.congr_fun hincl _
397	    rw [hcomp]
398	    exact hunit

\begin{lemma}[Base-change bridge to the localised cocycle ring homomorphisms]
  \label{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.baseChange_bridge}
  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_the_glue_data,
    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_incl_left,
    def:gr_away_incl_right}
  Fix indices \(I, J, K\) and the triple overlap \(V_{IJK}\) of the chart cover
  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}, \cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}).
  The scheme-pullback base-change map on global sections
  \(\Gamma(U^I_J, \mathcal{O}) \to \Gamma(V_{IJK}, \mathcal{O})\) induced by the first projection
  \(p^{IJ}_{IJK}\), and likewise the maps induced by the second projection and by the triple
  transition \(t'_{IJK}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}), are identified -- through the natural identification of global sections of an affine scheme
  with its coordinate ring and the fact that the projections and triple transition are the
  \(\Spec\) morphisms of the coordinate-ring homomorphisms by which the Grassmannian glue datum
  (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) is assembled -- with the ring homomorphisms
  \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}),
  \(\mathrm{awayInclRight}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right}) and \(\Theta_{IJ}\)
  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) over which the matrix cocycle
  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} is stated. Consequently the entrywise image of a Cramer
  inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) under the geometric base-change map agrees with its image under the
  corresponding coordinate-ring homomorphism, so \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} applies
  to the base-changed matrices.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij,
    def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_away_incl_right}
  The charts \(U^I\) and their overlaps are affine, identified with the spectra of the localised
  coordinate rings; under this identification the global-sections comorphism of a morphism of
  affine charts is the ring homomorphism that induces it on spectra. The triple overlap
  \(V_{IJK}\) is the spectrum of the triple-overlap localisation, and the two projections and
  the triple transition \(t'_{IJK}\) of the glue datum (\cref{def:gr_transition},
  \cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) are, on charts, the spectra of \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}\),
  \(\mathrm{awayInclRight}\) and \(\Theta_{IJ}\) respectively -- the precise form in which the
  chart transitions and glue datum are assembled from the transition maps. Applying the
  global-sections identification to each morphism translates its geometric base-change map on
  global sections into the named ring homomorphism, whence the entrywise images of the Cramer
  inverses coincide.
\end{proof}

% NOTE: forward declaration (planned C2 work); the \lean{} target baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec
% is not yet realised. Shared affine-chart identification underlying the three projection bridges.
\begin{lemma}[Affine global-sections comorphism is the inducing ring homomorphism]
  \label{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec}
  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme}
  Let \(\varphi : A \to B\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
  \(\Spec\varphi : \Spec B \to \Spec A\) the induced morphism of affine schemes. Under the
  natural identification of the global sections of an affine scheme with its coordinate ring
  -- the counit \(\Gamma(\Spec A, \mathcal O) \xrightarrow{\sim} A\) of the
  \(\Gamma \dashv \Spec\) adjunction, and likewise for \(B\) -- the global-sections base-change
  comorphism
  \[
    (\Spec\varphi)^{\sharp} : \Gamma(\Spec A, \mathcal O) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\Spec B, \mathcal O)
  \]
  is identified with \(\varphi\) itself; that is, the square relating
  \((\Spec\varphi)^{\sharp}\) to \(\varphi\) through the two counit isomorphisms commutes.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  This is the naturality of the counit isomorphism \(\Gamma \circ \Spec \cong \mathrm{id}\) of
  the global-sections / spectrum adjunction: for any ring homomorphism \(\varphi\) the comorphism
  of \(\Spec\varphi\) on global sections is, after transport through the natural counit
  isomorphisms at \(A\) and \(B\), the homomorphism \(\varphi\). No Grassmannian-specific data
  enters; the statement is the affine-scheme naturality square applied to a single ring map.
\end{proof}

  Fix indices \(I, J, K\) with triple overlap \(V_{IJK} = U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\) of the glue
  datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), and let
  \(p^{IJ}_{IJK} : V_{IJK} \to U^I_J\) be the first projection (the pullback projection of the
  chart inclusion \(\mathrm{chartIncl}\), \cref{def:gr_chart_incl}). Through the away-pullback
  identification \(V_{IJK} \cong \Spec R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)]\) (\cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso})
  the projection \(p^{IJ}_{IJK}\) is the \(\Spec\) morphism of the left away-inclusion
  \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}(P^I_J, P^I_K)\) (\cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst},
  \cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}). Consequently the global-sections base-change map of
  \(p^{IJ}_{IJK}\), transported through the affine global-sections identification, equals the
  ring homomorphism \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}(P^I_J, P^I_K)\); in particular the entrywise image of
  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) under the geometric base-change agrees with its image under
  \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst, def:gr_away_incl_left,
    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec}
  By \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst} the first pullback projection, precomposed with the
  away-pullback identification \cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}, equals
  \(\Spec(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}(P^I_J, P^I_K))\). The source and overlap charts are affine, so by
  \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec} the global-sections comorphism of this \(\Spec\)
  morphism is, through the affine global-sections identification, the ring homomorphism
  \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}(P^I_J, P^I_K)\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}). Applying the comorphism
  entrywise to \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) gives the asserted agreement.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Base-change bridge to the localised cocycle ring homomorphisms]
  \label{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge}

  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_the_glue_data,

    def:gr_away_incl_right, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left,
    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_right, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_transition}
  Fix indices \(I, J, K\) and the triple overlap \(V_{IJK}\) of the chart cover
  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}, \cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}).

  \(\Gamma(U^I_J, \mathcal{O}) \to \Gamma(V_{IJK}, \mathcal{O})\) induced by the first projection

  transition \(t'_{IJK}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}), are identified -- through the natural identification of global sections of an affine scheme

  \(\Spec\) morphisms of the coordinate-ring homomorphisms by which the Grassmannian glue datum
  (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) is assembled -- with the ring homomorphisms
  \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}),
  \(\mathrm{awayInclRight}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right}) and \(\Theta_{IJ}\)
  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) over which the matrix cocycle
  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} is stated. Consequently the entrywise image of a Cramer
  inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) under the geometric base-change map agrees with its image under the
  corresponding coordinate-ring homomorphism, so \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} applies
  to the base-changed matrices.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_right,
    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_transition}

  handled by \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left}, identifying its global-sections base-change
  map with \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}\); the second projection by
  \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_right}, identifying it with \(\mathrm{awayInclRight}\); and the
  triple transition \(t'_{IJK}\) by \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_transition}, identifying it
  with \(\Theta_{IJ}\). Each bridge already records the agreement of the entrywise image of the
  relevant Cramer inverse under the geometric base-change with its image under the named ring
  homomorphism over which \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} is stated, so assembling the three
  gives the claim.
\end{proof}
